title: Possession Without Protest Is No Waiver
description:
In a significant ruling on May 6, 2026, the Uttar Pradesh Real Estate Appellate Tribunal (UP REAT), in Uttar Pradesh Awas Vikas Parishad v. Real Estate Regulatory Authority, Lucknow & Ors., affirmed that a promoter’s obligation to pay delay interest under Section 18(1) of the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016 (RERA) is absolute, mandatory, and cannot be waived by an allottee accepting possession without formal protest.

The case involved an allottee who received flat possession from the Uttar Pradesh Awas Vikas Parishad over three years beyond the stipulated completion date. After an Adjudicating Officer (AO) granted delay interest, the promoter appealed, contending the AO lacked jurisdiction for such claims and that the allottee, by accepting possession without protest, had implicitly waived their right to delay interest.

The Tribunal concurred that the AO lacked jurisdiction, setting aside the impugned order. However, it utilized its powers to provide complete justice, directly addressing the substantive issue of waiver. UP REAT unequivocally rejected the promoter's waiver argument, emphasizing that the "shall" in Section 18(1) makes the payment of delay interest a self-executing, absolute right for the allottee. Accepting possession, without more, does not extinguish this statutory entitlement, as doing so would undermine RERA's protective framework and incentivize promoters to delay.

Ultimately, the Tribunal directed the promoter to pay delay interest at MCLR+1% per month on the deposited amount for the delayed period (August 27, 2015, to August 31, 2017) within 45 days. This judgment significantly reinforces homebuyer rights, establishing that delay interest under RERA is a mandatory and mathematical entitlement, not a discretionary concession subject to implicit waiver through the acceptance of possession.

title: Life Beyond Life: How the Supreme Court Granted Freedom to a Man Who Served Over Two Decades
description:
In a significant ruling, the Supreme Court of India in Munna Moyuddin Shaikh v. State of Gujarat (2026 INSC 558) exercised its inherent power to modify a sentence of life imprisonment. The Court reduced the sentence of Munna Moyuddin Shaikh, convicted for murder under Section 302 of the Indian Penal Code, to the period he had already served—over twenty-three years behind bars. This judgment, delivered by Justices K. V. Viswanathan and Vijay Bishnoi, clarified the scope of modified sentencing by constitutional courts.

Shaikh was convicted in 1998 by a Trial Court, a decision upheld by the Gujarat High Court in 2002. While the conviction itself was not seriously challenged before the Supreme Court, the core legal question revolved around the power of constitutional courts to modify a life sentence to a fixed term corresponding to the period already undergone, and whether this required prior notice to the accused.

The Court’s analysis drew upon crucial precedents: Union of India v. V. Sriharan affirmed that life imprisonment generally means natural life, but constitutional courts (High Court or Supreme Court) possess the power to impose modified fixed-term sentences. Shiva Kumar v. State of Karnataka expanded on this, stating that modifying a life sentence to a fixed term (not less than fourteen years) is a general sentencing tool, not restricted to cases involving the death penalty. Furthermore, Birbal Choudhary v. State of Bihar clarified that such modification constitutes a reduction, not an enhancement, of the sentence, thus obviating the need for prior notice under the Criminal Procedure Code.

Considering the appellant’s age at the time of the 1998 incident (approximately 21 years) and his actual imprisonment exceeding two decades, the Supreme Court upheld the concurrent findings on guilt. However, exercising its discretion, the Bench directed that the life sentence be reduced to the 23 years, 6 months, and 3 days already served, ordering Shaikh's immediate release unless required in other cases. This decision underscores the Supreme Court's role in ensuring humane and proportionate justice through a sensitive exercise of its wide sentencing powers.

title: Privacy at the Village Lake: Madras High Court Draws the Line on CCTV Surveillance
description:
The Madras High Court recently underscored the importance of privacy by refusing permission to install CCTV cameras around a village lake, even when proposed to prevent illegal activities like fishing and earth mining. In the case of R. Azhagappan v. The District Collector, the petitioner argued for surveillance to protect the water body.

However, the Court deemed the proposed surveillance a disproportionate intrusion on the daily lives and privacy of villagers, who use the lake for bathing, leisure, and social interaction. Referencing the landmark Justice K.S. Puttaswamy judgment, the Court affirmed that privacy, a fundamental right, extends to semi-private community spaces, particularly in rural settings where such areas are integral to the social fabric.

The judgment emphasized that while environmental protection is crucial, surveillance must satisfy the principle of proportionality and not be a default solution. Less intrusive alternatives, such as reporting illegal activities to authorities, should be utilized. This ruling significantly expands the dialogue on privacy to physical surveillance, acknowledges the unique nature of rural community spaces, and reinforces that technological measures must not override constitutional freedoms, thereby protecting individual dignity and autonomy.

title: Moratorium Under the IBC and the Commercial Wisdom of the CoC: Lessons from Davani Silks
description:
The NCLT Kochi Bench, in G. Vinayan v. C.S. Narendar Reddy Banala, issued a significant ruling on the application of Section 14 moratorium under the IBC and the commercial discretion of the Committee of Creditors (CoC) concerning leased premises. The case involved Davani Silks Pvt. Ltd., where the lease for commercial premises was mutually terminated before the Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP) commenced. Despite this, the Corporate Debtor (CD) retained physical possession, leading to rent arrears and financial hardship for the property owner (applicant) due to accumulating electricity charges and recovery proceedings.

The applicant sought immediate possession, contending that the terminated lease meant the premises were no longer CD assets and served no purpose given the CoC's decision to liquidate the company. The IRP, however, argued that physical possession and the presence of CD assets invoked the moratorium under Section 14, requiring CoC approval for vacation.

Referencing the Supreme Court's Sincere Securities judgment, the NCLT affirmed that while Section 14(1)(d) prohibits recovery of property occupied by a CD, the decision regarding the necessity of retaining such property falls squarely within the CoC's commercial wisdom. The Tribunal highlighted undisputed facts: the applicant's ownership, the mutual termination, and the CoC's resolution for liquidation. It further noted that rent during CIRP/liquidation constitutes priority costs, directly impacting the estate's financial resources and creditor recoveries.

Consequently, the NCLT held that the decision to retain or relinquish the premises resided solely with the CoC's commercial judgment. It directed the CoC to convene within 15 days to make an informed decision on the premises, considering the CD's financial standing and all stakeholders' interests. This ruling reinforces the primacy of the CoC's commercial wisdom and implicitly suggests that premises generating only liabilities without benefit should be relinquished, even when it means overriding third-party hardship in the short term.

title: Arbitrator’s Administrative Engagements Constitute Sufficient Cause for Extension Under Section 29A: Himachal Pradesh High Court
description:
The Himachal Pradesh High Court, in Sanjeev Kumar v. Union of India and Ors., held that an arbitrator's administrative preoccupations, arising from simultaneously holding a senior government office, constitute "sufficient cause" for extending the statutory time limit to pass an arbitral award under Section 29A of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996.

The applicant, Sanjeev Kumar, sought enhanced land acquisition compensation, with the Divisional Commissioner, Mandi, acting as the Arbitrator. Pleadings concluded on August 14, 2024, triggering the 12-month period under Section 29A(1). However, the Arbitrator's mandate expired on June 11, 2025, as proceedings could not be concluded due to his prior administrative engagements. This necessitated the applicant to approach the High Court for an extension.

The Court relied on Section 29A, which stipulates award delivery within 12 months from pleadings completion, extendable by consent or by court for "sufficient cause." Citing the Supreme Court's ruling in Rohan Builders (India) Private Limited, the High Court affirmed that such extension applications are maintainable even after the arbitrator's mandate has expired, provided sufficient cause is demonstrated.

Justice Virender Singh concluded that the Arbitrator's absence from scheduled hearings due to demanding administrative duties as Divisional Commissioner was an exigency beyond the ordinary control of the parties. This was deemed to squarely meet the definition of "sufficient cause" under Section 29A(5). Consequently, the High Court allowed the application, extended the Arbitrator's mandate, and directed the award to be passed by July 27, 2026.

title: 487 Days Too Late: The Haryana REAT’s Uncompromising Stance on Delay Condonation Under RERA
description:
The Haryana Real Estate Appellate Tribunal (REAT) recently underscored the non-negotiable demands of procedural discipline under RERA by dismissing an appeal filed a staggering 487 days beyond the statutory 60-day limit. This decision, involving appellants Pankaj Chawla and another against Amb Infraventures Pvt. Ltd., reinforces that the right to seek appellate redress is time-bound and requires due diligence.

The appellants sought condonation, citing their former counsel's failure to inform them of the Haryana RERA Authority's order, subsequent attempts to negotiate possession with the developer who later reneged, and delays incurred while engaging new counsel. However, the Tribunal applied principles from the Supreme Court's Pathapati Subba Reddy ruling, which emphasizes that while Section 5 of the Limitation Act allows for condonation, it cannot override the strict mandate of Section 3, particularly in cases of inordinate delay or negligence. The Supreme Court clarified that the merits of the underlying case are irrelevant to a delay condonation application.

The Tribunal deemed the appellants' explanations "circuitous" and insufficient, highlighting their failure to demonstrate that the delay was beyond their control or that they acted with reasonable promptness. It noted that RERA's 60-day limitation reflects a legislative intent for expeditious dispute resolution, which a 487-day unexplained deviation severely undermines. Consequently, the application for delay condonation and the appeal itself were dismissed.

This landmark decision serves as a crucial reminder for homebuyers and investors that RERA appeals are time-sensitive statutory remedies. Extra-judicial negotiations, while potentially promising, do not justify neglecting statutory deadlines. Litigants who allow limitation periods to lapse while pursuing informal settlements do so at their own risk, emphasizing that vigilance in exercising legal remedies is indispensable.

title: Reshaping India’s Insolvency Landscape: A Practitioner’s Guide to the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (Amendment) Act, 2026
description:
The Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (Amendment) Act, 2026, largely effective from May 26, 2026, represents the most significant legislative overhaul of India’s insolvency framework since its 2016 enactment. Addressing long-standing criticisms like procedural delays, ambiguities in avoidance transactions, and the absence of statutory frameworks for group or cross-border insolvency, this comprehensive Act introduces sweeping changes across the Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP), liquidation mechanics, adjudicatory timelines, and penalty structures.

Key amendments include a more prescriptive framework for admitting financial creditor applications under Section 7, mandating admission or rejection within 14 days. The withdrawal of admitted applications under Section 12A now requires a 90% Committee of Creditors (CoC) vote and is prohibited after the first invitation for resolution plans. Crucially, the Act extends moratorium principles to liquidation proceedings and empowers the CoC to opt for corporate debtor dissolution as a faster alternative to full liquidation. Furthermore, the CoC can now, with a 66% vote, seek a one-time restoration of the CIRP for up to 120 days before a liquidation order.

Significant reforms to liquidation include the mandatory separation of Resolution Professional and Liquidator roles, with the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Board of India (IBBI) nominating liquidators and the CoC gaining statutory supervisory powers and the ability to replace liquidators. Resolution plans must now provide dissenting financial creditors with at least the higher of their liquidation value or proportionate entitlement, and approved plans ensure the continuity of licenses and permits. The Act clarifies that prior claims against the debtor are extinguished post-approval, while explicitly preserving personal liability for promoters and guarantors. The look-back period for avoidance transactions is extended, and creditors now have express standing to report such transactions if the professional fails to act.

The penalty and enforcement architecture undergoes a major overhaul, replacing criminal offences with a comprehensive civil penalty regime under Section 235A. This allows for penalties up to three times the loss caused or unlawful gain, with a floor of Rs. 1 lakh per day and a cap of Rs. 5 crore for unquantifiable losses, aiming to reduce deterrent effects on good-faith commercial actors while maintaining enforcement capacity.

Provisions concerning a new creditor-initiated resolution framework, group insolvency, cross-border insolvency, and financial service providers are not yet in force, pending further regulatory groundwork. Overall, the Amendment Act consolidates judicial precedent, enhances enforcement through civil penalties, and lays the groundwork for future cross-border cooperation, necessitating immediate review by all stakeholders.

title: Rationalising Environmental Clearance Validity for Ports and Harbours: An Analysis of the Proposed Amendment to the EIA Notification, 2006
description:
India's Environment Impact Assessment (EIA) Notification, 2006, is undergoing a significant amendment via a draft notification dated May 20, 2026. This amendment proposes to rationalize the Environmental Clearance (EC) validity period for ports, harbours, breakwaters, and dredging projects, categorized under Item 7(e) of the Schedule.

Currently, ECs for these maritime projects are valid for 10 years, with a provision for a one-year extension, totaling 11 years. Industry stakeholders have consistently argued this duration is inadequate due to the projects' inherently extended gestation periods, phased development strategies, and frequent delays caused by factors like land acquisition, funding constraints, and macroeconomic fluctuations. Such issues often lead to EC expiration before project completion, necessitating burdensome reapplication or extension processes.

In response to these representations, the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change referred the matter to expert committees. Both the sectoral Expert Appraisal Committee and the Expert Advisory Committee independently recommended revising the EC validity to an initial 15 years, extendable by a further 5 years. They concurred that the existing framework was insufficient for the realities of port infrastructure development.

Following these recommendations, the Ministry has proposed amendments to Paragraph 9 of the EIA Notification, 2006. These changes aim to integrate ports, harbours, breakwaters, and dredging projects into the same EC validity framework that currently applies to nuclear power projects, granting them an initial 15-year validity with a maximum 5-year extension upon due examination.

The draft notification has been published and is open for a mandatory 60-day public consultation period, ensuring participatory oversight. This amendment is viewed as a judicious and evidence-based rationalization, designed to strike an appropriate balance between rigorous environmental diligence and the practical necessity of fostering sustainable infrastructure growth in India.

title: Departmental Proceedings Cannot Override POSH Procedure, MP High Court
description:
The Madhya Pradesh High Court, in Dr. Sajan Kurien Mathew v. State of Madhya Pradesh & Ors., has issued a significant ruling affirming that allegations of workplace sexual harassment must be exclusively inquired into by the Internal Complaints Committee (ICC) or Local Complaints Committee (LCC) established under the Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition and Redressal) Act, 2013 ("POSH Act"). This decision clarifies that parallel departmental inquiry mechanisms cannot override the statutory POSH procedure, making its framework mandatory rather than merely procedural.

The case involved an Assistant Professor whose services were terminated based on an inquiry conducted by a retired judicial officer, not an ICC, following sexual harassment allegations. The Court stressed that once allegations fall within the POSH Act's ambit, the disciplinary authority is "denuded" of power to appoint an independent Inquiry Officer. It extensively referenced the Vishaka Guidelines and the proviso to Rule 14(2) of the Madhya Pradesh Civil Services (CCA) Rules, 1966, which designates the ICC as the deemed inquiry authority in such matters.

Highlighting the importance of confidentiality and the specialized, gender-sensitive nature of the POSH mechanism, the Court distinguished it from other grievance committees like the Students’ Grievance Redressal Committee (SGRC). It ultimately quashed the petitioner's termination order, mandating a fresh inquiry by a duly constituted ICC. This judgment significantly reinforces the POSH Act's safeguards, preventing employers from circumventing its institutional framework and ensuring stricter compliance with its intended redressal mechanism.

title: Kerala High Court Clarifies Limits of Intermediary Liability and Writ Jurisdiction in Online Defamation Cases
description:
The Kerala High Court, in a significant judgment on May 22, 2026, in Anto Augustine v. Union of India, has delineated the boundaries of intermediary liability and writ jurisdiction regarding online defamation. The Court dismissed a petition seeking the removal of allegedly defamatory content on YouTube and compensation, asserting that intermediaries cannot be compelled to remove such content without a prior judicial declaration of its unlawful nature.

The ruling affirmed YouTube's status as an "intermediary" under the Information Technology Act, 2000, entitling it to safe harbour protections. Crucially, the Court reiterated the Supreme Court's Shreya Singhal precedent, codifying that "actual knowledge" triggering an intermediary's obligation to remove unlawful content arises solely upon receiving a court order or an authorized government notification, not merely from a private complaint or legal notice. The Court highlighted the legislative intent behind the removal of "defamatory" from the IT Rules, 2021, acknowledging that private intermediaries are institutionally incapable of adjudicating complex defamation claims.

Furthermore, the judgment underscored that Article 226 writ jurisdiction is unsuitable for fact-intensive defamation cases, which necessitate a detailed adjudication of falsity, malice, and harm—issues best resolved by civil courts that can provide remedies such as injunctive relief and damages. Free speech considerations also mandate a prior judicial finding of defamation before content can be restricted. A writ of mandamus against government authorities was also denied, as the petitioners had not formally approached the ministries with specific allegations of IT Rules non-compliance.

Practically, this judgment mandates that individuals aggrieved by online defamation must pursue civil litigation to obtain a court order, which then activates the intermediary's obligation to remove the content expeditiously. For digital intermediaries, the ruling reinforces their safe harbour, requiring action only upon judicial or authorized governmental directives, while emphasizing continuous compliance with other IT Rules. Regulators are expected to be engaged formally by stakeholders before courts intervene in enforcement matters.

This landmark ruling consolidates the intermediary liability framework, resisting the expansion of writ jurisdiction into contested factual determinations and reinforcing that private complaints alone are insufficient to displace intermediary safe harbour protections.

title: Statutory Tax Charges and the IBC Waterfall: The Madras High Court Ruling in Avenue Realty
description:
The Madras High Court, in Avenue Realty v. Assistant Commissioner, has significantly clarified the interplay between insolvency law and tax recovery, distinguishing corporate liquidation from resolution and applying a retrospective amendment to Section 3(31) of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (IBC). This decision addresses the scope of "secured creditor" status for tax authorities, particularly in light of the Supreme Court's Rainbow Papers ruling.

The case involved the liquidation of RLS Alloys, whose property, previously attached for tax arrears under the TNVAT Act, was purchased by Avenue Realty in an auction. The tax department's claims were rejected by the liquidator and dismissed by the NCLT for non-prosecution. A Single Judge, relying on Rainbow Papers, had held the tax department to be a "secured creditor," prompting the appeal.

Avenue Realty contended that Rainbow Papers was confined to the Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP) and didn't apply to liquidation. More critically, they cited the 2026 retrospective amendment to IBC Section 3(31), which clarifies that a "security interest" requires an agreement between parties, not merely operation of law. The Division Bench distinguished CIRP from liquidation, noting that Rainbow Papers didn't consider the IBC's Section 53 waterfall mechanism. The Court also highlighted the tax department's failure to prosecute its claims within the NCLT.

The Court held the 2026 amendment to be clarificatory and retrospective, explicitly negating the idea that statutory charges create a security interest under the IBC. Consequently, the tax department was not a secured creditor. The Bench allowed the appeal, setting aside the attachment and confirming the auction sale's validity. This ruling strongly reaffirms the IBC's primacy and the integrity of liquidation sales, limiting Rainbow Papers to its specific context and distinguishing consensual security interests from statutory tax charges.

title: The Interplay of Commercial Consistency and Undervalued Transactions: An Analysis of the NCLT Kochi Bench Judgment in Piyush Kisanlal Jani v. Chuzattil Naryanana Manoj
description:
The National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT) Kochi Bench recently dismissed an application by the Resolution Professional (RP) of Pelican Biotech and Chemicals Labs Private Limited, alleging undervalued transactions by its suspended directors. The RP’s audit report claimed the corporate debtor sold "COMPOSORB 100 LTR" to a related entity, M/s Pelican Kenterra Private Limited, at Rs. 300 per unit, significantly lower than the Rs. 862 per unit charged to third parties, resulting in a Rs. 5.56 lakh loss and violating IBC Section 45. The RP contended this price differential demonstrated an intent to confer undue benefit on a related party.

The suspended directors countered on several grounds. Procedurally, they argued the application was time-barred due to a 164-day delay, bypassing Regulation 35A, and challenged the non-joinder of M/s Pelican Kenterra Private Limited as a necessary party. Substantively, they claimed the transactions were arm's length, justified by their intellectual property rights and a royalty arrangement benefiting the corporate debtor. Crucially, they highlighted that the RP himself continued supplying the same product to M/s Pelican Kenterra Private Limited at the identical Rs. 300-400 per unit price during the Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP).

The NCLT dismissed the limitation objection, ruling that Regulation 35A timelines are directory, not mandatory, and a procedural delay cannot extinguish substantive rights. The non-joinder plea was also rejected, with the Tribunal classifying M/s Pelican Kenterra as a proper, not necessary, party since no direct adverse order was sought against it. On the merits, the NCLT found the RP’s continued adoption of the same pricing model post-CIRP compellingly negated the undervaluation claim. This commercial consistency indicated a legitimate, long-standing business arrangement rather than deliberate asset stripping.

The Tribunal concluded that the continuous transaction pattern disproved any intent to prejudice creditors. This judgment reinforces that avoidance applications cannot be based solely on theoretical price variances; commercial realities, consistent pricing structures, and independent business judgments, particularly when adopted by the RP himself, must be holistically evaluated before declaring transactions as undervalued under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code.

title: Section 34 of SARFAESI Bars Pre-Emptive Injunctions: Karnataka High Court Reaffirms Statutory Ouster
description:
The Karnataka High Court has definitively ruled that Commercial Courts exercising jurisdiction under Section 9 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, cannot issue interim injunctions restraining secured creditors from initiating proceedings under the Securitisation and Reconstruction of Financial Assets and Enforcement of Security Interest (SARFAESI) Act, 2002. This judgment, in Sammaan Finserve Ltd. v. Chimanlal Talreja, reinforces the robust statutory bar enshrined in Section 34 of the SARFAESI Act.

The dispute arose when a Commercial Court granted an interim injunction preventing appellants (secured creditors) from invoking SARFAESI, asserting that Section 34's ouster of civil court jurisdiction only applied once Debts Recovery Tribunal (DRT) jurisdiction was triggered after measures under Section 13(4) of SARFAESI had been taken. This created a temporal distinction, allowing pre-emptive intervention.

The High Court decisively rejected this interpretation. It clarified that Section 34 contains two limbs, with the second explicitly stating that "no injunction shall be granted by any court... in respect of any action taken or to be taken" under SARFAESI. The Court emphasized that "to be taken" unambiguously covers prospective enforcement actions, thereby prohibiting civil courts from staying SARFAESI proceedings even before they commence. This upholds SARFAESI's legislative intent for a swift, unhindered recovery mechanism.

This ruling significantly closes a legal gap, confirming that Section 34 forecloses civil court interference at every stage of the enforcement process. It curtails the use of arbitration proceedings under Section 9 of the A&C Act as a tactic to delay or forestall SARFAESI enforcement. The decision provides clear guidance: secured creditors can confidently challenge such injunctions, while Commercial Courts must recognize the limits of their jurisdiction when SARFAESI rights are involved. Borrowers are primarily directed to statutory remedies under Section 17 once enforcement actions are undertaken.

title: No Waiver of Pre-Deposit for Promoters: A Critical Analysis of DLF Ltd. v. Gautam Rana
description:
The Haryana Real Estate Appellate Tribunal, in DLF Ltd. v. Gautam Rana (01 May 2026), delivered a significant ruling reaffirming the mandatory pre-deposit requirement for promoters challenging refund orders under Section 43(5) of the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016 (RERA). This decision underscored the non-negotiable nature of this statutory obligation for promoters while upholding the constitutional validity of differential treatment between promoters and homebuyers.

The dispute arose when DLF Limited appealed an order from the Haryana Real Estate Regulatory Authority. The Authority had directed DLF to refund a deposited amount, along with interest, to an allottee whose allotment DLF had cancelled due to payment default. DLF, in its appeal filed with a significant delay, crucially failed to deposit the statutory pre-deposit. It sought a waiver, arguing the requirement was onerous and discriminatory compared to allottees, and that pre-deposit should not apply when the allottee was in breach.

The Tribunal unequivocally rejected DLF's contentions. Citing the Supreme Court's precedent in M/s Newtech Promoters and Developers Pvt. Ltd. v. State of UP, it held that under Section 43(5) of RERA, promoters appealing an order for monetary refunds must deposit the total amount, including interest, before the appeal can be entertained, and the Tribunal lacks jurisdiction to waive this mandate. Regarding the discrimination argument, the Tribunal found it legally unsustainable. It clarified that RERA’s framework imposes extensive obligations on promoters (Chapters III and VIII) while allottees have limited duties, forming an intelligible differentia aimed at protecting homebuyers. Thus, the differential treatment is neither arbitrary nor discriminatory.

Consequently, the Tribunal dismissed DLF's appeal as not maintainable due to the absence of the pre-deposit. However, it granted DLF one month to comply with the mandatory deposit to seek revival of the appeal. This judgment firmly reinforces the strictness of RERA's statutory framework, ensuring that legislative intent to safeguard allottee interests remains paramount and cannot be circumvented through procedural leniency by promoters.

title: Survival of Personal Guarantors’ Liability Post-Corporate Resolution Plan: An Analysis of the NCLAT Ruling in Amit Bhatnagar v. UCO Bank
description:
The NCLAT in Amit Bhatnagar v. UCO Bank provided critical clarity on personal guarantors' liability under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016. The ruling affirmed that such obligations can survive the approval of a resolution plan for a corporate guarantor, addressing the maintainability of Section 95 proceedings concerning limitation and the extinguishment of independent guarantor liability.

The case stemmed from personal guarantees securing credit facilities to a Corporate Debtor. Following the Corporate Debtor's liquidation and a DRT decree establishing the debt against both the Corporate Debtor and the personal guarantors, the corporate guarantor underwent its own Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP). A resolution plan for the corporate guarantor was subsequently approved. Although a consortium bank accepted payment under this plan as "full and final" for its claims within that specific CIRP, it later initiated Section 95 IBC proceedings against the personal guarantors for the outstanding balance.

The Appellants (personal guarantors) contended that the debt was extinguished by the corporate guarantor's resolution plan and the "full and final" settlement letter. They also argued the claims were time-barred from the original default date. Conversely, the banks maintained that personal guarantees were distinct, the settlement was limited to the corporate guarantor's CIRP, and the DRT decree provided a fresh, extended limitation period.

The NCLAT dismissed the appeals, upholding the NCLT's admission of the Section 95 petitions. It held that the DRT decree of February 6, 2020, served as an acknowledgment of debt, providing a fresh cause of action, which, combined with the Supreme Court's COVID-19 extension, rendered the Section 95 petitions timely. Crucially, relying on Lalit Kumar Jain v. Union of India, the Tribunal reiterated that a resolution plan's approval does not discharge a personal guarantor's liability. Partial recovery or settlement with one obligor does not extinguish the co-extensive liability of other obligors.

This judgment reinforces the enduring and independent nature of personal guarantee enforcement within India's insolvency framework, confirming that creditors retain the right to pursue guarantors for unpaid balances even after a corporate resolution.

title: Invoice Requirements in Section 9 Petitions: The NCLAT Ruling in Lyka Labs Ltd. v. Modi Lifecare Industries Ltd.
description:
The National Company Law Appellate Tribunal (NCLAT), in its judgment dated 19 May 2026 in Lyka Labs Ltd. v. Modi Lifecare Industries Ltd., significantly clarified the evidentiary requirements for operational creditors initiating insolvency under Section 9 of the IBC, 2016, especially where the underlying contract does not mandate invoicing. The case involved Lyka Labs (operational creditor) and Modi Lifecare (corporate debtor) under a Technical Guidance Agreement (2012) for royalty payments, which stipulated minimum guaranteed royalty amounts irrespective of actual sales and without requiring specific invoices for these guaranteed sums.

Lyka Labs issued a Form-3 demand notice for outstanding royalty without annexing invoices, as the agreement did not necessitate them for the minimum guaranteed royalty. Modi Lifecare disputed the claim, asserting royalty was only payable on actual sales based on an unformalised oral agreement, and contended the Form-3 notice was defective due to the absence of invoices. The NCLT twice rejected Lyka Labs' Section 9 petition, citing the non-annexation of invoices and the existence of a pre-existing dispute.

The NCLAT overturned the NCLT's decision, clarifying two crucial points. Firstly, it held that Rule 5 of the IBBI (Application to Adjudicating Authority) Rules, 2016, provides distinct alternatives: a demand notice in Form-3 or a copy of an invoice with a notice in Form-4. Therefore, invoices are not mandatory when Form-3 is used, particularly if the contract itself does not oblige their issuance for the debt in question, as was the case for the minimum guaranteed royalty. Secondly, regarding the "pre-existing dispute," the NCLAT found that Modi Lifecare's communication admitted a liability of Rs. 63 lakhs and conceded its oral agreement was never formalised. The Tribunal concluded that the dispute pertained merely to the quantum of debt and accounts reconciliation, not the existence of the operational debt itself.

The NCLAT allowed the appeal, affirming the existence of an operational debt and a default exceeding the statutory threshold. It directed Modi Lifecare to deposit Rs. 63,00,000 within 30 days. Failure to comply would empower the Adjudicating Authority to admit Modi Lifecare into the Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process under Section 9. This landmark ruling reinforces that the IBC's remedial framework is accessible for crystallised contractual debts and admitted defaults, even in the absence of conventional invoicing documentation, and provides essential guidance on distinguishing disputes of quantum from disputes regarding the existence of a debt.

title: Regulatory Compliance: Understanding CDSL’s KYC Validation Circular of May 2026
description:
Central Depository Services (India) Limited (CDSL) issued Circular No. CDSL/OPS/DP/POLCY/2026/325 on May 14, 2026, mandating immediate compliance from Depository Participants (DPs) for demat accounts with unvalidated Know Your Customer (KYC) records by KYC Registration Agencies (KRAs). This directive, a continuation of an earlier April 2026 circular, underscores SEBI’s commitment to purifying the securities market and maintaining the integrity of the Permanent Account Number (PAN).

The circular identifies two categories of non-compliance: invalid PANs and PANs updated between April 1-30, 2026, that subsequently failed KRA validation. CDSL has provided DPs with tailored lists of these deficient Beneficial Owner (BO) accounts. The most significant consequence is a complete freeze on both debit and credit transactions for all identified demat accounts, effective May 30, 2026, under Reason Code 27 (“Account holder related KYC non-compliant”). This freeze will be based on KRA data as of May 27, 2026, leaving a narrow window for resolution.

DPs are obligated to proactively identify affected BOs, engage with them to resolve KYC discrepancies, and ensure revised, validated KYC records are submitted to KRAs by May 27, 2026. Accounts frozen on May 30 will require DPs to follow prescribed unfreezing procedures. BOs with frozen accounts will be unable to execute any transactions until their KYC is remediated. Failure to comply exposes DPs to regulatory scrutiny and potential enforcement, highlighting the imperative for urgent and diligent action.

title: Acknowledgment of Debt and Limitation in SARFAESI Proceedings: DRAT Chennai Upholds Secured Creditor’s Enforcement Action
description:
The Debt Recovery Appellate Tribunal (DRAT), Chennai, in Sri Basavaraj S. Ankali v. LIC Housing Finance Ltd., delivered a pivotal ruling affirming that a borrower’s acknowledgment of debt extends the limitation period for initiating SARFAESI Act enforcement, even when the original default occurred over a decade prior. The case involved the second respondent (borrower) defaulting on a LIC Housing Finance Ltd. loan from October 2001, secured by an equitable mortgage created in May 2000. The property was subsequently sold to third parties, including the appellant, Sri Basavaraj S. Ankali, in 2004, while the mortgage was still active.

LIC Housing Finance Ltd. issued a Section 13(2) demand notice in June 2014, nearly thirteen years post-default. The appellant challenged this before the DRT and subsequently the DRAT, primarily arguing that the SARFAESI measures were time-barred under Article 62 of the Limitation Act, 1963. He contended that as a subsequent bona fide purchaser, unaware of the mortgage due to its absence from the encumbrance certificate, he could raise all available defences and was protected.

The financial institution countered, presenting borrower letters from July and November 2003 that explicitly acknowledged the debt and an intention to clear it by selling the property. The DRAT held these communications constituted a valid acknowledgment, extending the limitation period from July 2003, making the June 2014 notice timely. Regarding the appellant's bona fide purchaser claim, the Tribunal noted his admission that the original title deed was "misplaced," which should have prompted further inquiry. Crucially, an equitable mortgage by deposit of title deeds did not require registration at the time of its creation, rendering its absence from the encumbrance certificate irrelevant.

Consequently, the DRAT dismissed the appeal, upholding the DRT’s decision. This judgment underscores that a borrower's acknowledgment of debt significantly impacts limitation in SARFAESI proceedings, validating enforcement actions even after prolonged defaults. It also serves as a critical reminder to subsequent purchasers of mortgaged property regarding the paramount importance of due diligence, particularly when original title documents are unavailable, and that non-registration of equitable mortgages does not negate their validity.

title: Supreme Court: Residential Sale Deed of Adjoining Village Cannot Determine Compensation for Industrial Land Acquisition
description:
The Supreme Court has ruled that a residential sale deed from an adjoining village cannot be relied upon to determine compensation for industrial land acquired under the Right to Fair Compensation and Transparency in Land Acquisition, Rehabilitation and Resettlement Act, 2013 (2013 Act). The Court underscored the mandatory requirement of "similar type of land" under Section 26(1)(b) for assessing market value.

This judgment stemmed from the acquisition of 1,394 square metres of land in Nagpur for a National Highway expansion. While the Deputy Collector initially valued it as agricultural, the landowner, Alfa Remidis Ltd., argued industrial use. The Arbitrator and subsequently the High Court erred by adopting a residential sale deed rate of ₹3,588 per square metre from an adjoining village to compensate for the industrial land.

The Supreme Court, allowing the appeal by the National Highways Authority of India, held that the lower authorities failed to adhere to Section 26(1)(b), relying on a "totally dissimilar type of land." The Court clarified that compensation must be determined using the "highest of the average sale price" methodology, which necessitates multiple sale deeds, not a single transaction. It cited `Madhya Pradesh Road Development Corporation v. Vincent Daniel` to reinforce this principle.

Rejecting the residential sale deed benchmark of ₹3,588 per square metre, the Supreme Court accepted the Ready Reckoner valuation of ₹2,020 per square metre as the appropriate basis for compensation. The landowner will receive this revised compensation along with all consequential statutory benefits under the 2013 Act. This ruling reinforces the principle of fair valuation based on comparable land transactions involving similar nature and use.

title: Breaking the Barter: The Rajasthan High Court on Atta-Satta, Cruelty, and the Limits of Tradition
description:
The Rajasthan High Court, in Kiran Bishnoi v. Sunil Kumar (10 April 2026), allowed Kiran Bishnoi's appeal, dissolving her marriage under Section 13 of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955. This landmark judgment overturned a Family Court dismissal and unequivocally condemned the "Atta-Satta" (reciprocal exchange marriage) custom, particularly when involving minors.

Kiran Bishnoi had alleged severe matrimonial cruelty, including dowry demands, physical and sexual assault, and forced ousting. Her marriage was part of an Atta-Satta arrangement where her husband's minor sister was simultaneously married to Kiran's brother. The Family Court wrongly attributed the marital breakdown to this Atta-Satta conflict, specifically the sister's later refusal to cohabit, and deemed Kiran's departure voluntary, failing to independently assess the cruelty allegations.

The High Court rectified this, stating the Family Court erred by conflating the external Atta-Satta dispute with the independent inquiry into cruelty. It clarified that an individual's lawful personal choice, like the sister's refusal post-majority, cannot justify denying matrimonial relief. The court rejected "voluntary desertion," recognizing that duress often compels women to remain in abusive marriages. It affirmed that Kiran's legal recourse was a legitimate defensive measure, not misconduct, and cruelty must be established by a preponderance of probabilities.

Crucially, the High Court condemned Atta-Satta involving minors as gender coercion, a child rights violation, and familial extortion. Citing the Prohibition of Child Marriage Act, 2006, and principles of consent and dignity, the court highlighted that such arrangements treat girls as transactional instruments, making marriage "mutual hostage-taking" between families.

This judgment significantly contributes to matrimonial jurisprudence by emphasizing a holistic assessment of mental cruelty, not negated by co-residence. It clarifies that a wife's invocation of legal remedies reflects a breakdown of trust, and the standard of proof remains a preponderance of probabilities.

title: HDFC Bank Ltd. v. Rajsri Associates (HUF): DRAT Chennai on Attachment Before Judgment and Post-Order Conduct
description:
The Debt Recovery Appellate Tribunal (DRAT), Chennai, in HDFC Bank Ltd. v. Rajsri Associates (HUF), delivered a significant ruling on the scope of attachment before judgment (ABJ) under the Recovery of Debts Due to Banks and Financial Institutions Act, 1993. The judgment addressed whether new evidence of a defendant's intent to alienate secured assets, emerging after a Debt Recovery Tribunal (DRT) initially dismissed an ABJ application, could warrant reversing that dismissal.

HDFC Bank had filed an application for ABJ before DRT-II, Chennai, fearing the respondents might dispose of their properties. On September 24, 2025, the DRT dismissed the application, noting insufficient proof of mala fide intention by the respondents and their undertaking not to alienate assets, with original title deeds remaining with the bank. The DRT, however, directed the respondents to file a formal undertaking affidavit.

Crucially, following this DRT order, HDFC Bank discovered email communications between the first respondent and a prospective buyer actively negotiating the sale of scheduled properties. These emails, which included discussions on loan closure, were not presented to the DRT initially. The bank contended this fresh evidence demonstrated a clear attempt to violate the DRT's implied directions and dissipate assets. The respondents argued the negotiations were pre-existing and transparent, aimed at a one-time settlement, with one email even copied to the bank.

The DRAT, presided over by Mr. Justice G. Chandrasekharan, found these email communications constituted significant fresh material, revealing post-order conduct inconsistent with the respondents' assurances. Applying principles akin to Order 38, Rule 5 of the Code of Civil Procedure, the Tribunal concluded that actively negotiating asset sales despite judicial directions warranted interim protection.

Consequently, the DRAT allowed the appeal, setting aside the DRT-II's order. It remitted the ABJ application back to DRT-II for fresh consideration on merits, explicitly directing the DRT to remain uninfluenced by the appellate order. Pending final disposal of the application, both parties were directed to strictly adhere to the undertaking not to sell, mortgage, or encumber the scheduled properties. This judgment underscores the appellate forum's duty to consider new material revealing subsequent conduct that impacts the equities, ensuring effective remedies for secured creditors.

title: Daughters as Heirs: The Supreme Court’s Firm Stand on Interlocutory Res Judicata in Partition Suits
description:
In B.S. Lalitha & Others v. Bhuvanesh & Others (2026), the Supreme Court upheld Hindu daughters' inheritance rights, reversing a Karnataka High Court order. The Court clarified three issues: interlocutory res judicata on Order VII Rule 11 CPC pleas, Section 6(5) H.S. Act's nature as a saving clause, and daughters' independent Class I heir rights under Section 8 upon intestate death.

Daughters' partition suit contested a 2000 deed among brothers and mother, excluding them. An initial Order VII Rule 11 plea was dismissed by High Court (2013), maintaining the suit. Eight years later, a second application citing Vineeta Sharma as 'change in law' led High Court to reject the plaint, prompting this appeal.

The Supreme Court applied interlocutory res judicata, making the 2013 High Court decision binding. It dismissed arguments that different defendants or new grounds evaded res judicata, as parties shared common interests and grounds could have been raised earlier. Vineeta Sharma did not affect the 2013 order, based on daughters' existing Class I heir rights under Section 8, distinct from later coparcenary rights.

Section 6(5) H.S. Act was declared a saving clause for registered partitions before 2004, not a jurisdictional bar. The 2000 deed's disputed validity, a factual contention, couldn't be resolved summarily under Order VII Rule 11. Crucially, daughters' pre-existing right to their father's share as Class I heirs under Section 8, upon his 1985 intestate death, was affirmed, crystallizing independently of the 2005 Amendment and Section 6(5).

This judgment strengthens interlocutory res judicata, defines Section 6(5) as a merits defence, and upholds daughters' independent Section 8 succession rights, distinct from the 2005 Amendment.

title: Invoice Date as the Anchor: NCLT Ahmedabad on Foreign Currency Debt Conversion Under IBC Section 4
description:
The National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT) Ahmedabad, in PM Copper Wire & Cables BHD v. Relicab Cable Manufacturing Ltd., ruled that for determining the Section 4 minimum default threshold under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (IBC), foreign currency operational debt must be converted to Indian Rupees at the exchange rate prevailing on the invoice date. This date is deemed when liability crystallizes, explicitly rejecting the date of default or any subsequent date.

The case involved PM Copper Wire & Cables (Malaysian operational creditor) seeking to initiate CIRP against Relicab Cable Manufacturing Ltd. (Indian corporate debtor) for an outstanding USD 1,23,597.38. An earlier petition was dismissed because, at the then-prevailing exchange rate, the debt fell below the INR 1 Crore threshold. The current petition, filed nearly seven years after the invoice date (September 2019), attempted to meet the threshold by applying a significantly later RBI Reference Rate from January 2026.

The Tribunal emphatically held that jurisdiction under Section 4, being a statutory threshold, cannot be artificially created or retrospectively conferred through currency fluctuations. It stressed that the invoice date exchange rate (approximately INR 70.71 per USD on September 19, 2019) reflects the true, crystallized value of the debt. Using subsequent exchange rates would introduce uncertainty, making jurisdictional determination contingent on external economic variables, thereby defeating the IBC's objectives of certainty and time-bound resolution.

Consequently, the NCLT dismissed the petition as not maintainable, confirming that at the invoice date, the debt remained below the INR 1 Crore threshold. This judgment provides critical clarity for cross-border operational creditors, preventing the exploitation of currency volatility to establish IBC jurisdiction and ensuring the stability and predictability of the Code’s application.

title: Sale as the Sine Qua Non of RERA: Allahabad High Court Holds Leasehold Sub-Lease Projects Fall Outside the Ambit of the Real Estate Regulation Act
description:
The Allahabad High Court, in U.P. Real Estate Regulatory Authority v. Maa Bhagwati Commercial Reality N Resorts LLP, delivered a significant judgment on May 8, 2026, ruling that the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016 (RERA) applies solely to projects centered on the "sale" of plots, apartments, or buildings. The Court determined that developers operating on leasehold land with only sub-leasing rights are not obligated to register under RERA.

The case originated from a developer (respondent) who had leased agricultural land for 29 years and 11 months, with rights restricted to construction and sub-leasing. After the Uttar Pradesh RERA rejected its application for registration of a commercial project, the Appellate Tribunal directed registration. UP RERA then appealed to the High Court.

The Court thoroughly examined RERA's Preamble, Objects and Reasons, and the definitions of "promoter" (Section 2(zk)) and "real estate project" (Section 2(zn)). It concluded that the "sine qua non" for RERA's applicability is the explicit intention and legal right to "sell" units to allottees. As the respondent's lease deed only granted rights to construct and sub-lease, without any authority to sell, the fundamental prerequisite for RERA's jurisdiction was absent.

A decisive factor in the Court's ruling was UP RERA's own admission, contained in an affidavit, that the respondent was neither a "promoter" nor the project a "real estate project" under the Act. Citing established precedents on the binding nature of judicial admissions, the Court deemed this admission conclusive. Consequently, the High Court affirmed that the respondent was not compelled to seek RERA registration and ordered the Authority to withdraw any prohibitory communications.

This judgment significantly clarifies RERA's scope, confirming its regulatory reach over projects structured around the sale of immovable property, while excluding those based purely on leasehold sub-leasing arrangements where absolute ownership cannot be conveyed. It reinforces the importance of literal statutory interpretation and the weight of judicial admissions.

title: “Comedy of Errors” in Partition Litigation: Supreme Court Ends Procedural Deadlock in Execution Proceedings
description:
The Supreme Court, in Jennifer Messias v. Leonard G. Lobo, delivered a significant ruling clarifying the executability of decrees in partition suits, particularly distinguishing between preliminary and final decrees under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908. The judgment emphasizes adopting a substantive, justice-oriented interpretation of decrees over purely technical considerations, resolving a long-standing procedural deadlock.

The case involved a partition suit where the Trial Court's 2012 decree declared Jennifer Messias's one-half share in a flat, granted mesne profits, and directed a commissioner to effect partition or report its impracticability. Following the commissioner's report that the flat was indivisible, the Executing Court ordered a public auction. However, the High Court, adopting a technical view, held the 2012 decree was merely preliminary and thus unexecutable without a separately drawn final decree, thereby setting aside the execution proceedings.

The Supreme Court critically examined Section 2(2) and Order XX Rule 18 CPC, alongside relevant precedents, to assert that a decree's true nature is determined by its substantive contents and operative directions, not its label. It clarified that a decree, even if termed "preliminary," can possess attributes of a final decree and be executable if it conclusively determines the parties' rights and prescribes the mechanism for implementation, including sale of the property when physical partition is impracticable. The Court found the High Court's interpretation to be overly technical, leading to unnecessary procedural impediments.

Consequently, the Supreme Court set aside the High Court's order, restored the execution proceedings, and directed the expeditious completion of the auction process within three months. This landmark judgment reinforces the principle that procedural law must facilitate substantive justice, particularly in cases involving indivisible properties, and mandates trial courts to proactively advance partition suits to a final resolution, curbing protracted litigation.

title: Supreme Court Reaffirms Equitable Powers Under Section 28 of the Specific Relief Act: No Automatic Rescission for Delay in Deposit
description:
In Anand Narayan Shukla v. Jagat Dhari, the Supreme Court significantly reaffirmed the equitable nature of specific performance decrees under Section 28 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963. The Court clarified that a decree for specific performance does not automatically become inexecutable merely due to a purchaser's delay in depositing the balance sale consideration. Courts retain continuing supervisory jurisdiction and must adopt a justice-oriented, equitable approach, as rescission under Section 28 is discretionary, not automatic.

The case arose when an appellant (purchaser), despite a Trial Court decree to deposit consideration within one month, faced delays. This was due to the respondent's non-cooperation, their appeal (without a stay), and varying directions from the Execution Court. The appellant deposited the required amount upon explicit direction, yet lower courts rescinded the contract based on the initial delay.

The Supreme Court stressed that a specific performance decree is preliminary, keeping the court's jurisdiction active. Section 28 embodies equitable principles, mandating an evaluation of circumstances, parties' conduct, and bona fides. The Court criticized the lower courts' rigid application, noting the appellant's consistent readiness and willingness versus the respondent's actions and procedural ambiguities.

The ruling establishes key principles: courts possess ongoing power to extend time even after the expiry of the stipulated period, focusing on the decree-holder's wilful negligence rather than strict adherence to limitation laws. A holistic, equitable review is paramount. The Supreme Court overturned the lower courts' decisions, restoring the matter for fresh adjudication to ensure substantive justice prevails over hyper-technical interpretations.

title: Supreme Court: Residential Sale Deed of Adjoining Village Cannot Determine Compensation for Industrial Land Acquisition
description:
The Supreme Court has decisively ruled that a residential sale deed for land in an adjoining village cannot be used to determine compensation for industrial land acquired under the Right to Fair Compensation and Transparency in Land Acquisition, Rehabilitation and Resettlement Act, 2013 (2013 Act). The Court underscored the mandatory nature of Section 26(1)(b) of the 2013 Act, which requires compensation to be based on "similar type of land," clarifying that industrial and residential properties are not comparable.

This decision arose from the acquisition of 1,394 square metres of Alfa Remidis Ltd.'s land in Nagpur, used for industrial purposes, for a National Highway. The Arbitrator had mistakenly relied on a residential sale deed from an adjacent village, valuing the land at ₹3,588 per square metre, a valuation upheld by the Bombay High Court.

The Supreme Court, allowing the appeal, found that both the Arbitrator and High Court failed to adhere to the statutory mandate. It reiterated that determining the "average sale price" under Section 26(1)(b) necessitates considering multiple sale deeds, not a single transaction. Consequently, the Court rejected the residential sale deed benchmark and instead adopted the Ready Reckoner valuation of ₹2,020 per square metre as the appropriate compensation, in addition to all statutory benefits. This judgment reinforces the principle that land acquisition compensation must align with comparable transactions involving lands of similar nature and use to ensure fairness and consistency.

title: Supreme Court Directs Rajasthan to Introduce Rajasthani Language in Schools
description:
The Supreme Court has mandated the State of Rajasthan to formulate a comprehensive policy for introducing the Rajasthani language as a subject in all government and private schools. This significant judgment, delivered by Justices Vikram Nath and Sandeep Mehta, reinforces the constitutional importance of mother tongue-based education and aligns with the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020.

The Court emphasized that the constitutional vision for education in a child's mother tongue must be practically implemented, not remain merely symbolic. It directed Rajasthan to recognize Rajasthani as a local and regional language for educational purposes and progressively facilitate its use as a medium of instruction, initially at foundational stages and later at higher levels.

The bench criticized the State's "pedantic" argument that only languages listed in the Eighth Schedule of the Constitution are taught, highlighting that Rajasthani's existing academic recognition at the university level disproves claims of lacking pedagogical acceptance. The Court asserted its role in preventing the "stark dilution" of constitutional rights due to executive inaction, emphasizing that states bear a corresponding obligation to implement Union Government policies on mother tongue education.

This ruling, stemming from a Special Leave Petition challenging the Rajasthan High Court's decision, is a crucial development for the recognition and preservation of regional and mother tongue languages within India's educational system. Compliance is scheduled for September.

title: MahaRERA Order No. 65A/2026: Decommissioning the Legacy Portal and Mandating Migration to MahaCRITI
description:
MahaRERA Order No. 65A/2026, dated May 8, 2026, mandates the closure of its legacy 1.0 portal from May 11, 2026. This decisive step, taken under statutory provisions, aims to consolidate technological infrastructure and overcome operational inefficiencies caused by running dual IT systems. The new integrated platform, MahaCRITI (MahaRERA Complaint and Regulatory Integrated Technology Implementation), was launched in August 2024 and progressively expanded to include Project Lifecycle Management by May 2025, becoming the exclusive channel for all new project-related applications.

Despite a prolonged transition period exceeding one year and multiple extensions for rectifying deficiencies, a significant number of promoters failed to complete pending applications on the old system or update contact details, hindering administrative outreach. The continued parallel operation of both systems imposed substantial financial and administrative burdens. Therefore, MahaRERA has ordered the complete cessation of all submissions and processing on the legacy portal from May 10, 2026, 11:59 PM, making MahaCRITI the sole platform for regulatory compliance thereafter.

Crucially, the Order addresses the fate of incomplete applications on the old portal, mandating their administrative removal and closure. However, it explicitly clarifies that this closure is purely for administrative efficiency and does not constitute an adjudication on merits or a rejection of the applications. Promoters retain the right to submit fresh applications on the MahaCRITI platform, aligning with applicable procedures. This approach balances the Authority's modernization imperative with the protection of legitimate stakeholder interests, urging timely adaptation to the new digital processes for uninterrupted compliance.

title: Delegation Of Powers Under The Industrial Relations Code, 2020: An Analysis Of The Gazette Notification Dated 8th May, 2026
description:
The Industrial Relations Code, 2020 (IRC), effective November 21, 2025, consolidated three foundational labour statutes in India. On May 8, 2026, the Ministry of Labour and Employment issued Gazette Notification S.O. 2323(E), signifying a key administrative step by delegating Central Government powers under the IRC.

Utilizing Section 100 of the IRC, the notification delegates powers concerning Sections 78, 79, and 80 to Joint Secretary-level officers and above within the Ministry's Industrial Relations portfolio. This delegation mechanism is crucial for efficient, expert-level administration given India's industrial scale, ensuring consequential decisions are made by senior, accountable officers, without diluting statutory safeguards.

The delegated powers fall under Chapter X of the Code, applying to non-seasonal establishments employing 300+ workers. These provisions require prior government permission for lay-off (Section 78), set conditions for worker retrenchment (Section 79), and govern undertaking closure (Section 80).

Practically, the notification clarifies the competent authority for establishments under Central Government jurisdiction, streamlining the process by identifying specific senior officers for applications under these crucial sections. It also provides clarity for workers and unions on the designated officials for required hearings. This administrative measure is vital for the IRC's effective implementation, ensuring that decisions on matters significantly impacting employers and workers are handled by experienced, senior personnel, without altering substantive legal requirements.
